perturbation.original_text: A Business Day shall open at 8:00a.m. and close at 5:00p.m. Houston, Texas, time.
perturbation.changed_text: A Business Day shall open at 9:00a.m. and close at 4:00p.m. Houston, Texas, time.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'Business Day' can create uncertainty and potential conflicts with state labor laws regarding overtime calculations and when employees are considered to be working, as some laws specify or imply standard business hours.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FLSA does not explicitly define 'Business Day,' altering the defined hours affects calculations dependent on a standard workday, such as deadlines and overtime, potentially leading to violations if the altered definition conflicts with actual working hours and overtime requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Overtime Pay | U.S. Department of Labor Skip to main content Overtime Pay More in This Section Close Minimum Wage Overtime Pay Earnings thresholds for the Executive, Administrative, and Professional exemption from minimum wage and overtime protections under the FLSA Hours Worked Recordkeeping Federal Wage Garnishments Direct Care Workers On April 26, 2024, the U.S. Department of Labor (Department) published a final rule, Defining and Delimiting the Exemptions for Executive, Administrative, Professional, Outside Sales, and Computer Employees , to update and revise the regulations issued under section 13(a)(1) of the Fair Labor Standards Act implementing the exemption from minimum wage and overtime pay requirements for executive, administrative, and professional employees. Revisions included increases to the standard salary level and the highly compensated employee total annual compensation threshold, and a mechanism for updating these earnings thresholds to reflect current earnings data. On November 15, 2024, the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Texas vacated the Department’s 2024 final rule. Consequently, with regard to enforcement, the Department is applying the 2019 rule’s minimum salary level of $684 per week and total annual compensation requirement for highly compensated employees of $107,432 per year. Lawsuits regarding the 2024 final rule are currently pending in two other federal district courts, and the United States has filed a notice of appeal from the November 15 decision. The Department will update this notice with additional information as it becomes available. The federal overtime provisions are contained in the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA). Unless exempt, employees covered by the Act must receive overtime pay for hours worked over 40 in a workweek at a rate not less than time and one-half their regular rates of pay. There is no limit in the Act on the number of hours employees aged 16 and older may work in any workweek. The FLSA does not require overtime pay for work on Saturdays, Sundays, holidays, or regular days of rest, unless overtime is worked on such days. The Act applies on a workweek basis. An employee's workweek is a fixed and regularly recurring period of 168 hours — seven consecutive 24-hour periods. It need not coincide with the calendar week, but may begin on any day and at any hour of the day. Different workweeks may be established for different employees or groups of employees. Averaging of hours over two or more weeks is not permitted. Normally, overtime pay earned in a particular workweek must be paid on the regular pay day for the pay period in which the wages were earned. Guidance materials about overtime topics, including an Employment Law Guide, Qs & As, guide to overtime laws in the states, and more. Learn More about General Guidance for overtime Learn more by reading fact sheets that cover a variety of overtime topics. Learn More about Fact Sheets for overtime Access a collection of interactive online tools and presentations that address overtime pay requirements. Learn More about resources for overtime Every employer of employees subject to the Fair Labor Standards Act's minimum wage provisions must post, and keep posted, a notice explaining the Act in a conspicuous place in all of their establishments. Learn More about posters for overtime Review Administrator Interpretations, Opinion and Ruling Letters as well as pertinent chapters of the WHD Field Operations Handbook. Learn More about Interpretive Guidance for overtime Review the laws and regulations that govern overtime in the United States. Learn More about Laws and Regulations for overtime
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of 'Business Day' by shifting the opening and closing hours, which could create conflicts with overtime requirements under the FLSA. While the FLSA doesn't explicitly define a 'Business Day,' the scraped snippet regarding 'Overtime Pay' indicates that overtime is calculated based on hours worked over 40 in a workweek. By changing the defined business hours, the modified contract introduces potential ambiguity and miscalculation of overtime, especially if employees' actual working hours extend beyond the new definition of a 'Business Day.' The perturbation explanation mentions that the change can create uncertainty and potential conflicts with state labor laws regarding overtime calculations and when employees are considered to be working, as some laws specify or imply standard business hours. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because the altered definition of 'Business Day' can deviate from legally recognized definitions related to work hours and overtime, potentially making the contract unenforceable regarding overtime calculations.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party is responsible for payment and reporting of its federal, state, and local income taxes and state franchise, license, and similar taxes required for the maintenance of its business existence.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party is responsible for payment and reporting of its federal, state, and local income taxes, but the payment of state franchise, license, and similar taxes required for the maintenance of its business existence will be optional, at the Party's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This modification directly contradicts the requirement to pay state franchise taxes, potentially exposing the parties to penalties and legal action from the state. Franchise taxes are mandatory for maintaining a business's legal existence.
perturbation.law_explanation: Alabama law mandates the payment of franchise tax for corporations and LLCs operating in the state. Making this payment 'optional' directly violates this requirement, leading to non-compliance and potential penalties.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original contract by making the payment of state franchise, license, and similar taxes 'optional' instead of mandatory. The Perturbation Explanation clearly states that franchise taxes are mandatory for maintaining a business's legal existence. Since no scraped snippets were provided, the determination relies on the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and the direct comparison of the Original and Changed Text. This optionality directly undermines the legal obligation to pay these taxes, thus creating a legal contradiction under the definition of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this JSMA, including without limitation any question regarding its existence, validity or termination, that is not resolved in accordance with the provisions set forth below in Section 13.3, shall be submitted to the exclusive jurisdiction of the United States District Court for the Southern District of Alabama in the City of Mobile, unless that court does not have subject-matter jurisdiction or declines jurisdiction, in which case any such dispute shall be submitted to the exclusive jurisdiction of the State Court for the State of Alabama, City of Mobile, as described in the following paragraphs. Any counterclaim arising out of, or in connection with, the dispute shall be brought in the same proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this JSMA, including without limitation any question regarding its existence, validity or termination, that is not resolved in accordance with the provisions set forth below in Section 13.3, shall be submitted to a non-binding mediation in a location chosen by the party filing the suit, regardless of jurisdiction. Any counterclaim arising out of, or in connection with, the dispute shall be brought in a separate proceeding and in a jurisdiction chosen by the counterclaimant.
perturbation.explanation: This modification fundamentally alters the dispute resolution process. By making mediation non-binding and removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause, it undermines the enforceability of the original agreement and contradicts established principles of contract law regarding jurisdiction and dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FAA favors enforcing arbitration agreements. By replacing mandatory arbitration with non-binding mediation and altering jurisdictional rules, the modification diminishes the legal certainty and enforceability intended by the original clause and contradicts the FAA's purpose of promoting arbitration.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the exclusive jurisdiction of a specific court with non-binding mediation, contradicting the principle of enforcing arbitration agreements. Scraped Law Snippet 2 (9 U.S. Code § 2) states that agreements to arbitrate shall be "valid, irrevocable, and enforceable". The original text established a clear, enforceable jurisdiction, while the changed text undermines this by introducing non-binding mediation and allowing each party to choose jurisdictions for claims and counterclaims, weakening the enforceability of the original agreement. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.
